title: O-minimality and definable topologies
speaker: Margaret E M Thomas
abstract: From the outset, topology has played an important role in the study of o-minimal structures. The central focus has been on developing the theory of o-minimality as a framework for 'tame topology', built upon the natural and well-behaved underlying euclidean (order) topology. However, also vital in the development and wider application of o-minimality has been the study of topological objects that are definable in o-minimal structures. These include definable groups, with their natural definable manifold topology; the definable complex analytic spaces of Bakker--Brunebarbe--Tsimerman, which play a key role in applications to Hodge theory; and definable linear orders, function spaces and metric spaces. Our approach, over the past decade, has been to seek a more general understanding of the nature of topological spaces definable in o-minimal structures. This has led to various classification and embedding results, in particular for one-dimensional spaces, as well as analogues of topological properties suitable for the o-minimal setting (such as compactness, separability, first-countability etc.) and several applications. Some of the inspiration for these results comes from open conjectures in set-theoretic topology, although our methods are very much grounded in elementary o-minimal theory. This project is joint work with Andújar Guerrero, in part also with Walsberg, and is related to work carried out independently by Peterzil and Rosel.
tags: Workshop on Special Cycles and Related Topics

proof_stages.label: O-minimal definability framework
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes first-order structures via their definable sets, defines o-minimality by finite unions of points and intervals on the line, and surveys standard o-minimal sources.

proof_stages.label: Classical tameness and the definable-topology problem
proof_stages.summary: The Euclidean order topology supplies cell decomposition, monotonicity, finite connectedness, uniform finiteness, and dimension theory, motivating the general question of classifying topological spaces whose bases are definable families.

proof_stages.label: Finite palette of definable topological models
proof_stages.summary: A range of definable topologies is laid out, including Euclidean, half-open, discrete, split-interval, Alexander-type, nonregular, higher-dimensional, and metric constructions, to identify the model spaces and pathologies that later theorems must detect.

proof_stages.label: One-dimensional T1 basis and Hausdorff decomposition
proof_stages.summary: Using e-accumulation sets, a saturation argument, dimension bounds, left/right accumulation types, uniform finiteness, cell decomposition, and monotonicity, the talk proves that infinite one-dimensional T1 definable spaces contain an interval with one of four basic topologies, and Hausdorff spaces admit a finite partition into such pieces.

proof_stages.label: Regular one-dimensional embedding theorem
proof_stages.summary: For T3 one-dimensional definable topological spaces, the decomposition is refined into definable embeddings of finitely many pieces into lexicographic n-split interval spaces and Alexander-type spaces, parallel to known embeddings of definable linear orders.

proof_stages.label: Affineness via definable separability and compactification
proof_stages.summary: After replacing classical countability by a hereditary definable separability condition, the one-dimensional affine dichotomy is stated: either a bad half-open/discrete subspace appears or the space is definably homeomorphic to a Euclidean definable set, with quotient-space and compactification results as proof ingredients.

proof_stages.label: Set-theoretic consequences and higher-dimensional obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The classification yields definable analogues of set-theoretic topology results, but higher-dimensional counterexamples force a possible new program based on frontier-dimension conditions, definable complete regularity, and a conjectural definable metrization theorem via definable second countability.
